The provided R code is using plotly to generate graphics. It's creating two treemap plots side by side using two different data sets.

The first dataset is accessible via the provided URL and it's utilised to create the initial treemap. The key elements in the data set are 'ids', 'labels' and 'parents', which are used to define the hierarchy of the treemap.

The second treemap is generated from a different dataset, available via another URL. It uses similar key elements as the first treemap with the addition of 'maxdepth', which limits the tree's depth to one level.

Finally, the layout for the plot is set to a grid with two columns and one row, which arranges the two treemaps side by side. 

So, to recreate this figure, one would need both the datasets, create treemaps using the specified key elements, adjust the maxdepth for the second treemap, and layout the treemaps in the defined grid.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

df1 = read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/718417069ead87650b90472464c7565dc8c2cb1c/sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv')
df2 = read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/718417069ead87650b90472464c7565dc8c2cb1c/coffee-flavors.csv')

fig <- plot_ly(
  type='treemap',
  ids=df1$ids,
  labels=df1$labels,
  parents=df1$parents,
  domain=list(column=0))

fig <- fig %>% add_trace(
  type='treemap',
  ids=df2$ids,
  labels=df2$labels,
  parents=df2$parents,
  maxdepth=1,
  domain=list(column=1))
fig <- fig %>% layout(grid=list(columns=2, rows=1))

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import squarify
import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)

# Get leaf nodes (nodes that are not parents of any other node)
parents_set = set(df['parent'].dropna().unique()) if 'parent' in df.columns else set(df['parents'].dropna().unique())
parent_col = 'parent' if 'parent' in df.columns else 'parents'

leaves = df[~df['ids'].isin(parents_set)].copy()
sizes = [1] * len(leaves)
labels = leaves['labels'].fillna('').tolist()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 9))
colors = plt.cm.Spectral([i / max(len(sizes), 1) for i in range(len(sizes))])
squarify.plot(sizes=sizes, label=labels, color=colors, ax=ax,
              text_kwargs={'fontsize': 7}, pad=True)
ax.set_title("Coffee Flavors Treemap")
ax.axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()

The figure generated from the provided R code is a combination of three pie charts displayed as subplots. The data for these pie charts comes from the diamonds dataset, considering three variables: 'cut', 'color', and 'clarity'. 

Here are the crucial details of each of the pie charts:

1. The first pie chart named "Cut", represents the distribution of different cuts of diamonds. It uses variable 'cut' for labels and a calculated count 'n' for values. It occupies the left part of the plot grid from x=0 to x=0.4 and y=0.4 to y=1 on the grid.

2. The second pie chart named "Color", showcases the distribution of different colors of diamonds. It uses the variable 'color' for labels and a calculated count 'n' for values. It is located on the right part of the grid spanning from x=0.6 to x=1 and y=0.4 to y=1.

3. The third pie chart named "Clarity", displays the distribution of diamonds based on their clarity. It uses the variable 'clarity' for labels and a calculated count 'n' for values. This pie chart is located at the bottom part of the grid, with the x-values spanning from x=0.25 to x=0.75 and y=0 to y=0.6.

Overall, the entire plot presents no gridlines, no zero lines and no tick labels on both x and y axes. There is no legend provided. The title given to the subplot figure is "Pie Charts with Subplots". The pie charts within this figure provide an analysis of the diamond dataset in visual form, specifically concerning the distribution of values within the 'Cut', 'Color' and 'Clarity' variables.
code_reference:
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

fig <- plot_ly()
fig <- fig %>% add_pie(data = count(diamonds, cut), labels = ~cut, values = ~n,
          name = "Cut", domain = list(x = c(0, 0.4), y = c(0.4, 1)))
fig <- fig %>% add_pie(data = count(diamonds, color), labels = ~color, values = ~n,
          name = "Color", domain = list(x = c(0.6, 1), y = c(0.4, 1)))
fig <- fig %>% add_pie(data = count(diamonds, clarity), labels = ~clarity, values = ~n,
          name = "Clarity", domain = list(x = c(0.25, 0.75), y = c(0, 0.6)))
fig <- fig %>% layout(title = "Pie Charts with Subplots", showlegend = F,
         xaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE, zeroline = FALSE, showticklabels = FALSE),
         yaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE, zeroline = FALSE, showticklabels = FALSE))

The code is producing a donut chart using the Plotly library in R, with "mtcars" dataset.

The hole in the center of the donut chart is specified to be 60% of the pie's radius. This value can be adjusted according to preferences. 

The chart has been given a title: "Donut charts using Plotly". All grid lines, zero-lines, and tick labels for both x and y axes have been removed for aesthetic purposes. Also, the chart legend is not displayed. All these factors would need to be iterated while recreating the figure.

No specific random seed was used in the given code. So there is no requirement of a random seed for the recreation of this figure.
code_reference:
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

# Get Manufacturer
mtcars$manuf <- sapply(strsplit(rownames(mtcars), " "), "[[", 1)

df <- mtcars
df <- df %>% group_by(manuf)
df <- df %>% summarize(count = n())
fig <- df %>% plot_ly(labels = ~manuf, values = ~count)
fig <- fig %>% add_pie(hole = 0.6)
fig <- fig %>% layout(title = "Donut charts using Plotly",  showlegend = F,
                      xaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE, zeroline = FALSE, showticklabels = FALSE),
                      yaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE, zeroline = FALSE, showticklabels = FALSE))

The x-axis of the chart represents the name of the products - 'Product A', 'Product B', and 'Product C'. The y-axis corresponds to the values 20, 14, and 23 respectively for these products. 

Each bar in the chart also carries a text annotation showing the market share of each product, with 'Product A' having '27% market share', 'Product B' having '24% market share' and 'Product C' having '19% market share'.

library(plotly)

x <- c('Product A', 'Product B', 'Product C')
y <- c(20, 14, 23)
text <- c('27% market share', '24% market share', '19% market share')
data <- data.frame(x, y, text)

fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~x, y = ~y, type = 'bar', text = text,
        marker = list(color = 'rgb(158,202,225)',
                      line = list(color = 'rgb(8,48,107)',

         xaxis = list(title = ""),
         yaxis = list(title = ""))

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

products = ['Product A', 'Product B', 'Product C']
values = [20, 14, 23]
shares = ['27% market share', '24% market share', '19% market share']

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
bars = ax.bar(products, values, color=(158/255, 202/255, 225/255),
              edgecolor=(8/255, 48/255, 107/255), linewidth=1.5)

for bar, share in zip(bars, shares):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height()/2, share,
            ha='center', va='center')

ax.set_title("January 2013 Sales Report.")
plt.show()

3. X-axis - 'cut' of the diamonds

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

cuts = ['Fair', 'Good', 'Very Good', 'Premium', 'Ideal']
clarities = ['I1', 'SI2', 'SI1', 'VS2', 'VS1', 'VVS2', 'VVS1', 'IF']

counts = {
    'I1':    [210, 96, 84, 205, 146],
    'SI2':   [466, 1081, 2100, 2949, 2598],
    'SI1':   [408, 1560, 3240, 3575, 4282],
    'VS2':   [261, 978, 2591, 3357, 5071],
    'VS1':   [170, 648, 1775, 1989, 3589],
    'VVS2':  [69, 286, 1235, 870, 2606],

}

The figure displays a grouped bar chart designed with plotly in R. The bar chart shows data from years 1995 to 2012. It compares the US export of plastic scrap to two categories, "China" and "Rest of the World" (roW). These are represented in the figure with different colored bars.

The x-axis is representing the years ('x') from 1995 to 2012 which is not titled, while the y-axis labeled as 'USD (millions) represents the export amount in millions of dollars.

The bar chart is organized into groups that correlate to each year, with each group containing two bars, one for China and another for the rest of the world. The bar representing "Rest of the World" is assigned the colour "rgb(55, 83, 109)" and the bar for "China" uses the colour "rgb(26, 118, 255)". 

The title of the chart is 'US Export of Plastic Scrap', and the legend uses a white, transparent background. Other styling details include specified bargap and bargroupgap properties, as well as detailed font style for both axis titles and tick labels. 

Regarding the data values - for the "China" category, the US export values start at 16 million in 1995 and ultimately reach 499 million in 2012. For "Rest of the World", the values start at 219 million in 1995 and ultimately reach 439 million in 2012.

library(plotly)

instruction:
The R code provided describes the process to create a stack bar plot that represents the annual profit for the year 2015. It depicts three types of values: revenue, costs, and profit across seven different categories. These categories are Product Revenue, Services Revenue, Total Revenue, Fixed Costs, Variable Costs, Total Costs, and Total. 

Values for each category are provided in the form of monetary amounts (in thousands) like $430K, $260K, $690K, -$120K, -$200K, -$320K, $370K. The bar plot is composed of three layers or traces: one for revenue (shown in blue color), one for costs (shown in red color), and the last one for profit (shown in green color).

There are no axis titles, and the background color of both the plot and the paper is light gray (RGB code: 245, 246, 249). There's no legend displaying information about the three data traces.

Additionally, the monetary amounts related to each category are displayed in the plot corresponding to their values. This is done through annotations, displayed in white color, using 'Arial' font and size 14. There is no arrow indicating these annotations in the stacked bar.

Recreating this figure requires the specific color codes represented in RGBA format, font specifications, the specific layout details mentioned, and the data for the seven categories in the respective format. The figure doesn't include implications of any random seed for its reproduction.

library(plotly) x <- c('Product
Revenue', 'Services
Revenue', 'Total
Revenue', 'Fixed
Costs', 'Variable
Costs', 'Total
Costs', 'Total') y <- c(400, 660, 660, 590, 400, 400, 340) base <- c(0, 430, 0, 570, 370, 370, 0) revenue <- c(430, 260, 690, 0, 0, 0, 0) costs <- c(0, 0, 0, 120, 200, 320, 0) profit <- c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 370) text <- c('$430K', '$260K', '$690K', '$-120K', '$-200K', '$-320K', '$370K') data <- data.frame(x, base, revenue, costs, profit, text) #The default order will be alphabetized unless specified as below: data$x <- factor(data$x, levels = data[["x"]]) fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~x, y = ~base, type = 'bar', marker = list(color = 'rgba(1,1,1, 0.0)')) fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~revenue, marker = list(color = 'rgba(55, 128, 191, 0.7)', line = list(color = 'rgba(55, 128, 191, 0.7)', width = 2))) fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~costs, marker = list(color = 'rgba(219, 64, 82, 0.7)', line = list(color = 'rgba(219, 64, 82, 1.0)', width = 2))) fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~profit, marker = list(color = 'rgba(50, 171, 96, 0.7)', line = list(color = 'rgba(50, 171, 96, 1.0)', width = 2))) fig <- fig %>% layout(title = 'Annual Profit - 2015', xaxis = list(title = ""), yaxis = list(title = ""), barmode = 'stack', paper_bgcolor = 'rgba(245, 246, 249, 1)', plot_bgcolor = 'rgba(245, 246, 249, 1)', showlegend = FALSE) fig <- fig %>% add_annotations(text = text, x = x, y = y, xref = "x", yref = "y", font = list(family = 'Arial', size = 14, color = 'rgba(245, 246, 249, 1)'), showarrow = FALSE) fig

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

midwest = sns.load_dataset('midwest') if 'midwest' in sns.get_dataset_names() else None
if midwest is None:
    import pandas as pd, numpy as np
    states = ['IL', 'IN', 'MI', 'OH', 'WI']

    for s in states:

The visualization being created in this script is a sunburst diagram. The names to be displayed in the chart are "Eve", "Cain", "Seth", "Enos", "Noam", "Abel", "Awan", "Enoch", and "Azura". These are represented by the 'labels'. 

The corresponding 'parent' relationship of each name is referred to as "", "Eve", "Eve", "Seth", "Seth", "Eve", "Eve", "Awan", "Eve". In a sunburst diagram, empty quotes denote that the label doesn't have a parent or it is at the root level. The rest of the labels are children nodes linked back to their parent node. 

The 'values' represent the size of the area covered by each label in the figure and in this case, the amounts are 65, 14, 12, 10, 2, 6, 6, 4, 4 respectively. 

The 'branchvalues' indicator is set to 'total', which means that the area (or angle, depending on the layout) of each sector corresponds to the total values of the 'values' array.

No random seed is used here. To recreate this visual, you'll need the names, parent-child relationship, and the corresponding values of area coverage.

library(plotly)

  labels = c("Eve", "Cain", "Seth", "Enos", "Noam", "Abel", "Awan", "Enoch", "Azura"),
  parents = c("", "Eve", "Eve", "Seth", "Seth", "Eve", "Eve", "Awan", "Eve"),

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

labels = ["Eve","Cain","Seth","Enos","Noam","Abel","Awan","Enoch","Azura"]
parents = ["","Eve","Eve","Seth","Seth","Eve","Eve","Awan","Eve"]
values = [65,14,12,10,2,6,6,4,4]

children = {}
for l,p in zip(labels,parents):
    children.setdefault(p,[]).append(l)
val = dict(zip(labels,values))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,8), subplot_kw=dict(polar=True))
ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
ax.axis("off")

cmap = plt.cm.tab20
color_map = {l: cmap(i % 20) for i,l in enumerate(labels)}

def draw(node, level, start, end):
    if level > 0:
        width = end - start
        ax.bar(x=start+width/2, height=1, width=width, bottom=level,
               color=color_map[node], edgecolor='white', align='center')
        ax.text(start+width/2, level+0.5, node, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9)
    kids = children.get(node, [])
    if not kids:
        return
    total = val[node] if level>0 else sum(val[k] for k in kids)
    cur = start
    span = end - start
    ksum = sum(val[k] for k in kids)
    for k in kids:
        frac = val[k]/ksum * span if ksum else 0
        draw(k, level+1, cur, cur+frac)
        cur += frac

draw("", 0, 0, 2*np.pi)
ax.set_ylim(0, 5)
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

regions = ['North America', 'Europe', 'Australia', 'South America', 'Africa', 'Asia']
children = {
    'North America': ['Football', 'Soccer'],
    'Europe': ['Soccer', 'Rugby'],
    'Australia': ['Rugby', 'Aussie Rules', 'Soccer'],
    'South America': ['Soccer'],
    'Africa': ['Soccer', 'Rugby'],
    'Asia': ['Soccer'],
}

region_values = {r: len(children[r]) for r in regions}
total = sum(region_values.values())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8), subplot_kw=dict(polar=True))
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
ax.set_theta_zero_location('N')
ax.axis('off')

cmap = plt.cm.tab10
colors_region = {r: cmap(i) for i, r in enumerate(regions)}

start = 0
for i, r in enumerate(regions):
    width = 2 * np.pi * region_values[r] / total
    ax.bar(start + width/2, 1, width=width, bottom=0, color=colors_region[r],
           edgecolor='white', align='center')
    ax.text(start + width/2, 0.5, r, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9, color='white')
    cs = children[r]
    cstart = start
    for c in cs:
        cw = width / len(cs)
        ax.bar(cstart + cw/2, 1, width=cw, bottom=1,
               color=colors_region[r], alpha=0.6, edgecolor='white', align='center')
        ax.text(cstart + cw/2, 1.5, c, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=8)
        cstart += cw
    start += width

ax.set_ylim(0, 2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

instruction:
The figure is generated with data from a CSV file available at the specified URL. The data is of coffee flavors and contains the following columns: ids, labels, and parents. 

The figure is a Sunburst diagram, a one-level deep hierarchical chart known for its radial layout. It is used to understand the proportion of different parts in hierarchical data. The ids are given to nodes which help uniquely identify them, labels are given names to be visible on the chart, and parents determine the relationships between nodes.

The orientation of the text inside the plot is radial, which means it follows a circular path. Its maximum depth is 2, only allowing two levels of branches from the center. This visualization is divided into different sections as per the columns 'ids', 'labels', 'parents' from the data. 

Remember that the location where the figure will be displayed will be in the first column and the figure's maximum depth is 2, indicating that it will only show two layers from the root. The Sunburst chart also has a radial orientation for the insidetext, providing better readability. 

To sum up, a Sunburst chart is created with coffee flavor data from the CSV file. Please be noted that this information should help us recreate the same or a similar figure in a different environment or for a different dataset with similar properties.
code_reference:
df = read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/718417069ead87650b90472464c7565dc8c2cb1c/coffee-flavors.csv')

fig <- plot_ly()

fig <- fig %>% add_trace(
  type='sunburst',
  ids=df$ids,
  labels=df$labels,
  parents=df$parents,
  domain=list(column=1),
  maxdepth=2,
  insidetextorientation='radial'
)
fig
code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/coffee-flavors.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)

ids = df['ids'].astype(str).tolist()
labels = df['labels'].astype(str).tolist()
parents = df['parents'].fillna('').astype(str).tolist()

children = {}
for i, p in zip(ids, parents):
    children.setdefault(p, []).append(i)

label_map = dict(zip(ids, labels))

roots = children.get('', [])

def depth_of(node, d=0):
    ch = children.get(node, [])
    if not ch:
        return d
    return max(depth_of(c, d+1) for c in ch)

def leaves_count(node, max_depth, cur=0):
    ch = children.get(node, [])
    if not ch or cur >= max_depth:
        return 1
    return sum(leaves_count(c, max_depth, cur+1) for c in ch)

MAX_DEPTH = 2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 10), subplot_kw=dict(projection='polar'))

cmap = plt.get_cmap('tab20')
root_colors = {r: cmap(i % 20) for i, r in enumerate(roots)}

def draw(node, theta0, theta1, level, root):
    if level > MAX_DEPTH:
        return
    if level > 0:
        color = root_colors[root]
        alpha = max(0.3, 1.0 - (level-1)*0.25)
        width = 1
        ax.bar((theta0+theta1)/2, width, width=(theta1-theta0),
               bottom=level, color=color, alpha=alpha, edgecolor='white', linewidth=1)
        ang = np.degrees((theta0+theta1)/2)
        rot = ang - 90 if ang <= 180 else ang + 90
        ax.text((theta0+theta1)/2, level+0.5, label_map.get(node, ''),
                ha='center', va='center', rotation=rot, fontsize=7)
    ch = children.get(node, [])
    if not ch or level >= MAX_DEPTH:
        return
    total = sum(leaves_count(c, MAX_DEPTH-level-1) for c in ch)
    cur = theta0
    for c in ch:
        frac = leaves_count(c, MAX_DEPTH-level-1) / total
        nxt = cur + (theta1-theta0)*frac
        draw(c, cur, nxt, level+1, root if root else c)
        cur = nxt

total_leaves = sum(leaves_count(r, MAX_DEPTH-1) for r in roots)
cur = 0
for r in roots:
    frac = leaves_count(r, MAX_DEPTH-1) / total_leaves
    nxt = cur + 2*np.pi*frac
    draw(r, cur, nxt, 0, r)
    cur = nxt

ax.set_ylim(0, MAX_DEPTH+1)
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
ax.set_theta_offset(np.pi/2)
ax.axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()

instruction:
The figure generated by the provided R code is a double-column sunburst plot that visualizes hierarchical data of coffee flavors. This figure is created using two CSV data sets: 'coffee-flavors.csv' and 'sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv' from the plotly datasets. 

The plot has two parts. The first part of the sunburst plot considers two levels of hierarchy (maxdepth = 2) and is located in the first column. The second part of the sunburst plot considers three levels of hierarchy (maxdepth = 3) and is located in the second column. 

The design of the figure includes a grid layout with two columns, and the margins have been minimized to allow more space for the plot. The color scheme of the figure includes 10 different colors, with extended colors based on these. The exact color values are provided in a hexadecimal format and are used uniformly across the figure.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

d1 <- read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/coffee-flavors.csv')
d2 <- read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/718417069ead87650b90472464c7565dc8c2cb1c/sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv')
fig <- plot_ly() 
fig <- fig %>%
  add_trace(
    ids = d1$ids,
    labels = d1$labels,
    parents = d1$parents,
    type = 'sunburst',
    maxdepth = 2,
    domain = list(column = 0)
    ) 
fig <- fig %>%
  add_trace(
    ids = d2$ids,
    labels = d2$labels,
    parents = d2$parents,
    type = 'sunburst',
    maxdepth = 3,
    domain = list(column = 1)
  ) 
fig <- fig %>%
    layout(
      grid = list(columns =2, rows = 1),
      margin = list(l = 0, r = 0, b = 0, t = 0),

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

base_colors = ['#1f77b4','#ff7f0e','#2ca02c','#d62728','#9467bd',
               '#8c564b','#e377c2','#7f7f7f','#bcbd22','#17becf']

# Simulated hierarchical coffee flavor data
# Level 1: main categories
cats = ['Floral','Fruity','Sour/Fermented','Green/Vegetative','Roasted',
        'Spices','Nutty/Cocoa','Sweet','Other']
cat_sizes = [3, 5, 4, 4, 4, 3, 3, 4, 2]

# Level 2: subcategories per category
subs = {
    'Floral': ['Black Tea','Floral-Other','Chamomile'],
    'Fruity': ['Berry','Dried Fruit','Citrus','Other Fruit','Stone Fruit'],
    'Sour/Fermented': ['Sour','Alcohol','Fermented','Winey'],
    'Green/Vegetative': ['Olive Oil','Raw','Green','Beany'],
    'Roasted': ['Pipe Tobacco','Tobacco','Burnt','Cereal'],
    'Spices': ['Pungent','Pepper','Brown Spice'],
    'Nutty/Cocoa': ['Nutty','Cocoa','Almond'],
    'Sweet': ['Brown Sugar','Vanilla','Overall Sweet','Sweet Aromatics'],
    'Other': ['Papery','Chemical'],
}

# Level 3: leaves per subcategory (1-3 each)
np.random.seed(0)
leaves = {}
for c, sl in subs.items():
    for s in sl:
        leaves[s] = [f'{s}-{i+1}' for i in range(np.random.randint(1,4))]

def sunburst(ax, maxdepth, title):
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.axis('off')
    total = sum(cat_sizes)
    # Inner ring
    start = 90
    for i, (c, sz) in enumerate(zip(cats, cat_sizes)):
        ang = sz/total*360
        color = base_colors[i % len(base_colors)]
        wedge = plt.matplotlib.patches.Wedge((0,0), 1, start-ang, start,
                                              facecolor=color, edgecolor='white', linewidth=1.5)
        ax.add_patch(wedge)
        mid = np.deg2rad(start - ang/2)
        ax.text(0.5*np.cos(mid), 0.5*np.sin(mid), c, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=7)
        # Second ring
        sub_start = start
        sublist = subs[c]
        for j, s in enumerate(sublist):
            sang = ang/len(sublist)
            scolor = color
            w2 = plt.matplotlib.patches.Wedge((0,0), 1.7, sub_start-sang, sub_start,
                                               width=0.7, facecolor=scolor, edgecolor='white',
                                               linewidth=1, alpha=0.75)
            ax.add_patch(w2)
            mid2 = np.deg2rad(sub_start - sang/2)
            ax.text(1.35*np.cos(mid2), 1.35*np.sin(mid2), s, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=5)
            if maxdepth >= 3:
                leaf = leaves[s]
                lstart = sub_start
                for k, lf in enumerate(leaf):
                    lang = sang/len(leaf)
                    w3 = plt.matplotlib.patches.Wedge((0,0), 2.3, lstart-lang, lstart,
                                                       width=0.6, facecolor=scolor,
                                                       edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.5)
                    ax.add_patch(w3)
                    lstart -= lang
            sub_start -= sang
        start -= ang
    r = 2.4 if maxdepth >= 3 else 1.75
    ax.set_xlim(-r, r); ax.set_ylim(-r, r)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6))
plt.subplots_adjust(left=0.02, right=0.98, top=0.95, bottom=0.02, wspace=0.05)
sunburst(axes[0], 2, 'Coffee Flavors (maxdepth=2)')
sunburst(axes[1], 3, 'Coffee Flavors (maxdepth=3)')
plt.show()

The figure being generated here is showing gender disparities in salaries across different schools. The data used here for recreating the figure is sourced from a csv file named 'school_earnings.csv'. 

The graphical representation primarily involves:
1. Sorting data based on men's incomes.
2. Setting up the general plot with gray color.
3. Adding segments, which would represent the salary differences from women to men in each school.
4. Adding markers for the women's salaries colored pink.
5. Adding markers for the men's salaries colored blue.

Key specifications of the diagram layout include a title that is "Gender earnings disparity", a title for the x-axis that is "Annual salary (in thousands)", and a left margin with a length of 65. To generate the same figure, the same data source and plotting specifications have to be used.

Please note, the colors used for the plot are 'gray80' for general, and 'pink' and 'blue' for the marker points for women's and men's salaries respectively. The absence of a legend is also notable.
code_reference:
s <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv")
# order factor levels by men's income (plot_ly() will pick up on this ordering)
s$School <- factor(s$School, levels = s$School[order(s$Men)])

library(plotly)
fig <- plot_ly(s, color = I("gray80"))
fig <- fig %>% add_segments(x = ~Women, xend = ~Men, y = ~School, yend = ~School, showlegend = FALSE)
fig <- fig %>% add_markers(x = ~Women, y = ~School, name = "Women", color = I("pink"))
fig <- fig %>% add_markers(x = ~Men, y = ~School, name = "Men", color = I("blue"))
fig <- fig %>% layout(
    title = "Gender earnings disparity",
    xaxis = list(title = "Annual Salary (in thousands)"),
    margin = list(l = 65)
  )

The figure created in this R code is a Gantt Chart which uses the dataset located at "https://cdn.rawgit.com/plotly/datasets/master/GanttChart-updated.csv". Here's the important information about recreating this figure:

1. Data: The data used for this chart is a CSV file loaded from a link. The CSV file seems to contain the following key columns: Start, Duration, Task, and Resource.

2. Dates: The 'Start' column in the data file, indicating the start date of a task, is converted to date format.

3. Color: The color of each task line in the chart is chosen based on the number of unique resources available in the dataset.

4. Tasks: Each unique task in the dataset results in a separate line or "trace" on the Gantt chart. These tasks are represented as thick lines on the chart.

5. Custom Hover Text: When you hover over each task line on the Gantt chart, you see information about the task, its duration in days and the resource associated with it. 

6. X-axis: The x-axis represents the time (date). The chart is coded to handle these dates automatically.

Remember that each trace is essentially a thick line plot and each task is a separate trace. The x-axis ticks, which are dates, are handled automatically.
code_reference:
library(plotly) # Read in data df <- read.csv("https://cdn.rawgit.com/plotly/datasets/master/GanttChart-updated.csv", stringsAsFactors = F) # Convert to dates df$Start <- as.Date(df$Start, format = "%m/%d/%Y") # Sample client name client = "Sample Client" # Choose colors based on number of resources cols <- RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(length(unique(df$Resource)), name = "Set3") df$color <- factor(df$Resource, labels = cols) # Initialize empty plot fig <- plot_ly() # Each task is a separate trace # Each trace is essentially a thick line plot # x-axis ticks are dates and handled automatically for(i in 1:(nrow(df) - 1)){ fig <- add_trace(fig, x = c(df$Start[i], df$Start[i] + df$Duration[i]), # x0, x1 y = c(i, i), # y0, y1 mode = "lines", line = list(color = df$color[i], width = 20), showlegend = F, hoverinfo = "text", # Create custom hover text text = paste("Task: ", df$Task[i], "
", "Duration: ", df$Duration[i], "days
", "Resource: ", df$Resource[i]), evaluate = T # needed to avoid lazy loading ) } fig
code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import timedelta

url = "https://cdn.rawgit.com/plotly/datasets/master/GanttChart-updated.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)
df['Start'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Start'])
df['Finish'] = df['Start'] + pd.to_timedelta(df['Duration'], unit='D')

resources = df['Resource'].unique()
cmap = plt.get_cmap('tab10')
colors = {r: cmap(i % 10) for i, r in enumerate(resources)}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
tasks = df['Task'].tolist()
for i, row in df.iterrows():
    ax.hlines(y=i, xmin=row['Start'], xmax=row['Finish'],
              color=colors[row['Resource']], linewidth=10,
              label=row['Resource'])

ax.set_yticks(range(len(df)))
ax.set_yticklabels(df['Task'])
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlabel('Date')
ax.set_title('Gantt Chart')

handles, labels = ax.get_legend_handles_labels()
by_label = dict(zip(labels, handles))
ax.legend(by_label.values(), by_label.keys(), title='Resource', loc='best')

fig.autofmt_xdate()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

tasks = [
    ("Task 1", 0, 5, "Resource A"),
    ("Task 2", 3, 7, "Resource B"),
    ("Task 3", 6, 12, "Resource C"),
    ("Task 4", 10, 15, "Resource A"),
    ("Task 5", 14, 20, "Resource B"),
]

resource_colors = {
    "Resource A": "#264E86",
    "Resource B": "#74A9CF",
    "Resource C": "#A6BDDB",
}

client_name = "ACME Corp"
total_duration = max(t[2] for t in tasks) - min(t[1] for t in tasks)
total_resources = len(set(t[3] for t in tasks))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

for i, (task, start, end, resource) in enumerate(tasks):
    ax.barh(i, end - start, left=start, color=resource_colors[resource], edgecolor='black')

ax.set_yticks(range(len(tasks)))
ax.set_yticklabels([t[0] for t in tasks])
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlabel("Time")

handles = [mpatches.Patch(color=c, label=r) for r, c in resource_colors.items()]
ax.legend(handles=handles, loc='lower right')

fig.text(0.80, 0.10,
         f"Total Duration: {total_duration}\nTotal Resources: {total_resources}",
         color='#264E86', fontsize=12, ha='center')

fig.text(0.1, 0.97, f"Gantt Chart: {client_name}",

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import numpy as np

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/CollegeGenderGap.csv"
try:
    df = pd.read_csv(url)
except Exception:
    states = ['Massachusetts','California','Pennsylvania','New Jersey','Illinois','Washington DC',
              'Connecticut','New York','North Carolina','New Hampshire','Indiana','Michigan',
              'Rhode Island','Georgia']
    np.random.seed(0)
    n = 60
    df = pd.DataFrame({
        'School': [f'U{i}' for i in range(n)],
        'Women': np.random.uniform(30000, 90000, n),

        'State': np.random.choice(states, n)
    })

codes = states.cat.codes
colors = [cmap(c % 12) for c in codes]

gap = df['Gap'].astype(float)
gmin, gmax = gap.min(), gap.max()
smin, smax = 10, 50
sizes = smin + (gap - gmin) / (gmax - gmin) * (smax - smin)
sizes = np.pi * (sizes/2)**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9,6))

ax.grid(False)

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminderDataFiveYear.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)
df = df[df['year'] == 2007]

continent_colors = {
    'Africa': '#1f77b4',
    'Americas': '#ff7f0e',
    'Asia': '#2ca02c',
    'Europe': '#d62728',
    'Oceania': '#9467bd'
}

slope = 2.666051223553066e-05

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))
fig.patch.set_facecolor((243/255, 243/255, 243/255))
ax.set_facecolor((243/255, 243/255, 243/255))

for continent, group in df.groupby('continent'):
    sizes = group['pop'] * slope * 100
    ax.scatter(group['gdpPercap'], group['lifeExp'],
               s=sizes, c=continent_colors.get(continent, 'gray'),
               edgecolors='white', linewidths=2, alpha=0.85,
               label=continent)

ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlim(10**2.003297660701705, 10**5.191505530708712)
ax.set_ylim(36.12621671352166, 91.72921793264332)
ax.set_xlabel('Per Capita GDP (2000 dollars)')
ax.set_ylabel('Life Expectancy (years)')
ax.set_title('Life Expectancy v. Per Capita GDP, 2007')
ax.grid(True, color='white', linewidth=2)
ax.axhline(0, color='white', linewidth=1)
ax.axvline(0, color='white', linewidth=1)
ax.tick_params(length=5)
for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_visible(False)
ax.legend(title='Continent', loc='lower right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import plotly.graph_objects as go

))

))

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

groups = ['Control', 'Experimental']
trials = ['Trial 1', 'Trial 2', 'Trial 3']
control = [3, 6, 4]
control_err = [1, 0.5, 1.5]
exp = [4, 7, 3]
exp_err = [0.5, 1, 2]

x = np.arange(len(groups))
width = 0.25

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for i, trial in enumerate(trials):
    vals = [control[i], exp[i]]
    errs = [control_err[i], exp_err[i]]
    ax.bar(x + (i-1)*width, vals, width, yerr=errs, label=trial, capsize=5)

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(groups)
ax.legend()
plt.show()

The first one, 'sinc(x)', is a theoretical data series, depicted as a line graph, formed by generating an array of 100 evenly spaced numbers between -4 and 4, and applying the sinc function to these values. 

The second series, 'measured', is depicted as a scatter plot with error bars. This plot consists of two lists of numbers. The error bars associated with the 'measured' plot have been described to be purple in color, 1.5 units thick, and 3 units wide. The error in y-direction has a constant value of 0.1 units, and in the x-direction, the error has a constant value of 0.2 units.

import numpy as np

))

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px

df = px.data.tips()
x = df['total_bill'].values
y = df['tip'].values

bins = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 31)
idx = np.digitize(x, bins) - 1
idx = np.clip(idx, 0, len(bins)-2)
means = np.array([y[idx==i].mean() if np.any(idx==i) else 0 for i in range(len(bins)-1)])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,6))
ax.bar(bins[:-1], means, width=np.diff(bins), align='edge', edgecolor='white', color='#636EFA')
ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')
ax.set_ylabel('avg of tip')
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

df = px.data.tips()

sexes = ['Male', 'Female']
smokers = ['Yes', 'No']
colors = {'Male': '#636EFA', 'Female': '#EF553B'}
hatches = {'Yes': '//', 'No': ''}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, sharey=True, figsize=(10, 5))
bins = np.linspace(df['total_bill'].min(), df['total_bill'].max(), 20)

for ax, sex in zip(axes, sexes):
    bottom = np.zeros(len(bins) - 1)
    for smoker in smokers:
        data = df[(df['sex'] == sex) & (df['smoker'] == smoker)]['total_bill']
        counts, _ = np.histogram(data, bins=bins)
        ax.bar(bins[:-1], counts, width=np.diff(bins), bottom=bottom,
               color=colors[sex], hatch=hatches[smoker], edgecolor='white',
               align='edge', label=f'{sex}, smoker={smoker}')
        bottom += counts
    ax.set_title(f'sex={sex}')
    ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')

axes[0].set_ylabel('count')
fig.legend(loc='center right')
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0.82, 1])
plt.show()

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = px.data.tips()
bins = 8
counts, edges = np.histogram(df['total_bill'], bins=bins)
sums, _ = np.histogram(df['total_bill'], bins=bins, weights=df['tip'])

centers = (edges[:-1] + edges[1:]) / 2
widths = np.diff(edges)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
bars = ax.bar(centers, avgs, width=widths, edgecolor='white', color='#636EFA')
for bar, v in zip(bars, avgs):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height(), f'{v:.2f}',
            ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

instruction: The given code is utilizing Plotly to generate two overlaying histograms with reduced opacity of 0.75 for better clarity. The data for these histograms are sourced from two different Gaussian distributions. The first histogram uses a standard Gaussian distribution, and the second one is derived from a Gaussian distribution which has its mean shifted by 1. instruction:
The figure is a plot created using `plotly.graph_objects`, specifically it's a double histogram plot, displaying two different sets of random data. 

The first histogram utilizes 500 points of standard normally distributed data (mean=0, standard deviation=1), and the second histogram uses an additional 500 data points from a normal distribution, but they're shifted by 1 unit to the right in this case. 

Both histograms are normalized by a percentage for easier comparison. 

The first histogram ('control') has bins with a size of 0.5, ranging from -4.0 to 3.0, and is displayed in a pink color ('#EB89B5') with 75% opacity. 

The second histogram ('experimental') also has bins with a 0.5 size, but they range from -3.0 to 4.0, and are displayed in a purple color ('#330C73') with 75% opacity. 

The plot is equipped with a title 'Sampled Results.' The x-axis is labeled 'Value' while the y-axis is labeled 'Count.' 

There is a gap of 0.2 between bars of adjacent location coordinates and a gap of 0.1 between bars of the same location coordinates. 

To recreate this plot, you would need to generate the same data sets, plot them as histograms with the specified attributes, and design the layout according to the specified design above. 

Please note, this code does not involve a seed for the random number generator, thus, the data will be different every time the code is run.
code_reference:
import plotly.graph_objects as go

import numpy as np
x0 = np.random.randn(500)
x1 = np.random.randn(500) + 1

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=x0,
    histnorm='percent',
    name='control', # name used in legend and hover labels
    xbins=dict( # bins used for histogram
        start=-4.0,
        end=3.0,
        size=0.5
    ),
    marker_color='#EB89B5',
    opacity=0.75
))
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=x1,
    histnorm='percent',
    name='experimental',
    xbins=dict(
        start=-3.0,
        end=4,
        size=0.5
    ),
    marker_color='#330C73',
    opacity=0.75
))

fig.update_layout(
    title_text='Sampled Results', # title of plot
    xaxis_title_text='Value', # xaxis label
    yaxis_title_text='Count', # yaxis label
    bargap=0.2, # gap between bars of adjacent location coordinates
    bargroupgap=0.1 # gap between bars of the same location coordinates
)

fig.show()

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

x = ['1970-01-01', '1970-01-01', '1970-02-01', '1970-04-01', '1970-01-02',
     '1972-01-31', '1970-02-13', '1971-04-19']

fig = make_subplots(rows=3, cols=2)

trace0 = go.Histogram(x=x, nbinsx=4)
trace1 = go.Histogram(x=x, nbinsx = 8)
trace2 = go.Histogram(x=x, nbinsx=10)
trace3 = go.Histogram(x=x,
                      xbins=dict(
                      start='1969-11-15',
                      end='1972-03-31',
                      size='M18'), # M18 stands for 18 months
                      autobinx=False
                     )
trace4 = go.Histogram(x=x,
                      xbins=dict(
                      start='1969-11-15',
                      end='1972-03-31',
                      size='M4'), # 4 months bin size
                      autobinx=False
                      )
trace5 = go.Histogram(x=x,
                      xbins=dict(
                      start='1969-11-15',
                      end='1972-03-31',
                      size= 'M2'), # 2 months
                      autobinx = False
                      )

fig.append_trace(trace0, 1, 1)
fig.append_trace(trace1, 1, 2)
fig.append_trace(trace2, 2, 1)
fig.append_trace(trace3, 2, 2)
fig.append_trace(trace4, 3, 1)
fig.append_trace(trace5, 3, 2)

fig.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

dates = pd.to_datetime(['1970-01-01', '1970-01-01', '1970-02-01', '1970-04-01',
                        '1970-01-02', '1972-01-31', '1970-02-13', '1971-04-19'])
data = dates.values.astype('datetime64[D]').astype(float)

start = np.datetime64('1969-11-15').astype('datetime64[D]').astype(float)
end = np.datetime64('1972-03-31').astype('datetime64[D]').astype(float)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 2, figsize=(10, 10))

axes[0, 0].hist(data, bins=4)
axes[0, 1].hist(data, bins=8)
axes[1, 0].hist(data, bins=10)

def month_bins(months):
    bins = []
    cur = pd.Timestamp('1969-11-15')
    end_ts = pd.Timestamp('1972-03-31')
    while cur <= end_ts:
        bins.append(cur)
        cur = cur + pd.DateOffset(months=months)
    bins.append(cur)
    return np.array([np.datetime64(b.date()).astype('datetime64[D]').astype(float) for b in bins])

axes[1, 1].hist(data, bins=month_bins(18))
axes[2, 0].hist(data, bins=month_bins(4))
axes[2, 1].hist(data, bins=month_bins(2))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

tips = px.data.tips()
days_order = ['Thur', 'Fri', 'Sat', 'Sun']

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))

bottoms = {d: 0 for d in days_order}

    if d in bottoms:

ax1.set_xlabel('day')
ax1.set_ylabel('tip')
ax1.set_title('Stacked Bar Chart - Hover on individual items')

ax2 = axes[1]
sums = [tips[tips['day'] == d]['tip'].sum() for d in days_order]
ax2.bar(days_order, sums, color='steelblue')
ax2.set_xlabel('day')
ax2.set_ylabel('sum of tip')
ax2.set_title('Histogram Chart')

ax.set_ylabel('sum of tip')
ax.set_title('Histogram Chart')

plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle, PathPatch
from matplotlib.path import Path
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter

hair = ['Black','Black','Black','Brown','Brown','Brown','Red','Brown']
eye = ['Brown','Brown','Brown','Brown','Brown','Blue','Blue','Blue']
sex = ['Female','Female','Female','Male','Female','Male','Male','Male']

dims = [('Hair', hair), ('Eye', eye), ('Sex', sex)]
n = len(hair)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
x_positions = [0, 1, 2]
bar_width = 0.05

cat_positions = []
for di, (name, vals) in enumerate(dims):
    cats = list(dict.fromkeys(vals))
    counts = Counter(vals)
    total = sum(counts.values())
    gap = 0.02
    y = 0
    pos = {}
    for c in cats:
        h = counts[c] / total * (1 - gap * (len(cats)-1))
        pos[c] = (y, y + h)
        ax.add_patch(Rectangle((x_positions[di]-bar_width/2, y), bar_width, h,
                               color='gray'))
        ax.text(x_positions[di], y + h/2, c, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=10)
        y += h + gap
    cat_positions.append(pos)

colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, n))

for di in range(len(dims)-1):
    left_used = {c: cat_positions[di][c][0] for c in cat_positions[di]}
    right_used = {c: cat_positions[di+1][c][0] for c in cat_positions[di+1]}
    _, lvals = dims[di]
    _, rvals = dims[di+1]
    for i in range(n):
        lc, rc = lvals[i], rvals[i]
        lspan = cat_positions[di][lc]
        rspan = cat_positions[di+1][rc]
        lh = (lspan[1]-lspan[0]) / Counter(lvals)[lc]
        rh = (rspan[1]-rspan[0]) / Counter(rvals)[rc]
        ly0 = left_used[lc]; left_used[lc] += lh
        ry0 = right_used[rc]; right_used[rc] += rh
        x0 = x_positions[di]+bar_width/2
        x1 = x_positions[di+1]-bar_width/2
        xm = (x0+x1)/2
        verts = [(x0,ly0),(xm,ly0),(xm,ry0),(x1,ry0),
                 (x1,ry0+rh),(xm,ry0+rh),(xm,ly0+lh),(x0,ly0+lh),(x0,ly0)]
        codes = [Path.MOVETO]+[Path.CURVE4]*3+[Path.LINETO]+[Path.CURVE4]*3+[Path.CLOSEPOLY]
        ax.add_patch(PathPatch(Path(verts,codes), facecolor=colors[i], alpha=0.5, edgecolor='none'))

for di,(name,_) in enumerate(dims):
    ax.text(x_positions[di], 1.05, name, ha='center', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 2.3); ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.12); ax.axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.sankey import Sankey
import numpy as np

hair = ["Black", "Brown", "Brown", "Brown", "Red"]
eye = ["Brown", "Brown", "Brown", "Blue", "Blue"]
sex = ["Female", "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male"]
counts = [6, 10, 40, 23, 7]

dims = [hair, eye, sex]
dim_names = ["Hair", "Eye", "Sex"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

total = sum(counts)
x_positions = [0, 1, 2]
bar_width = 0.08

category_positions = {}

for i, (dim, name) in enumerate(zip(dims, dim_names)):
    unique_cats = []
    cat_counts = {}
    for v, c in zip(dim, counts):
        if v not in cat_counts:
            cat_counts[v] = 0
            unique_cats.append(v)
        cat_counts[v] += c

    y = 0
    category_positions[i] = {}
    for cat in unique_cats:
        h = cat_counts[cat] / total
        ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x_positions[i] - bar_width/2, y), bar_width, h,
                                     facecolor='gray', edgecolor='black'))
        ax.text(x_positions[i], y + h/2, f"{cat}", ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9)
        category_positions[i][cat] = [y, y + h, y]
        y += h + 0.01
    ax.text(x_positions[i], -0.05, name, ha='center', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(counts)))

src_offsets = {i: {cat: category_positions[i][cat][0] for cat in category_positions[i]} for i in range(3)}

for idx, (h_v, e_v, s_v, cnt) in enumerate(zip(hair, eye, sex, counts)):
    frac = cnt / total
    vals = [h_v, e_v, s_v]
    for i in range(2):
        src_cat = vals[i]
        dst_cat = vals[i+1]
        y0 = src_offsets[i][src_cat]
        y1 = src_offsets[i+1][dst_cat]
        x0 = x_positions[i] + bar_width/2
        x1 = x_positions[i+1] - bar_width/2
        xs = np.linspace(x0, x1, 50)
        t = (xs - x0) / (x1 - x0)
        smooth = 3*t**2 - 2*t**3
        ys_low = y0 + (y1 - y0) * smooth
        ys_high = ys_low + frac
        ax.fill_between(xs, ys_low, ys_high, color=colors[idx], alpha=0.5)
        src_offsets[i][src_cat] += frac
        src_offsets[i+1][dst_cat] += frac

ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 2.3)
ax.set_ylim(-0.1, 1.1)
ax.axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

tips = sns.load_dataset('tips')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

colors = {'Male': '#636EFA', 'Female': '#EF553B'}

for sex in tips['sex'].unique():
    sub = tips[tips['sex'] == sex].sort_values('total_bill')
    x = sub['total_bill'].values
    y = sub['tip'].values
    order = np.argsort(x)
    x_sorted = x[order]
    y_cum = np.cumsum(y[order])
    x_plot = np.concatenate([[x_sorted[0]], np.repeat(x_sorted[1:], 2), [x_sorted[-1]]])
    y_plot = np.concatenate([np.repeat(y_cum[:-1], 2), [y_cum[-1], y_cum[-1]]])
    ax.plot(x_sorted, y_cum, drawstyle='steps-post', label=str(sex), color=colors.get(sex))

ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')
ax.set_ylabel('tip')
ax.legend(title='sex')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px

df = px.data.tips()

times = sorted(df['time'].unique())
days = ['Thur', 'Fri', 'Sat', 'Sun']
sexes = df['sex'].unique()
colors = {sexes[0]: '#636EFA', sexes[1]: '#EF553B'}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(times), len(days), figsize=(14, 7), sharex=True, sharey=True)

for i, t in enumerate(times):
    for j, d in enumerate(days):
        ax = axes[i, j]
        sub = df[(df['time'] == t) & (df['day'] == d)]
        for s in sexes:
            vals = np.sort(sub[sub['sex'] == s]['total_bill'].values)
            if len(vals) == 0:
                continue
            y = np.arange(1, len(vals)+1) / len(vals)
            ax.plot(vals, y, color=colors[s], label=s)
        if i == 0:
            ax.set_title(f'day={d}')
        if j == len(days)-1:
            ax.text(1.02, 0.5, f'time={t}', transform=ax.transAxes, rotation=-90, va='center')
        if i == len(times)-1:
            ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')
        if j == 0:
            ax.set_ylabel('ECDF')

handles = [plt.Line2D([0],[0], color=colors[s], label=s) for s in sexes]
fig.legend(handles=handles, title='sex', loc='center right')
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0,0,0.92,1])
plt.show()

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

try:
    from plotly.data import gapminder
    df = gapminder()
except Exception:
    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminder_with_codes.csv"
    df = pd.read_csv(url)

years = sorted(df['year'].unique())
continents = sorted(df['continent'].unique())
colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(continents)))
color_map = dict(zip(continents, colors))

ncols = 4
nrows = int(np.ceil(len(years) / ncols))
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows, ncols, figsize=(4*ncols, 3*nrows), sharex=True, sharey=True)
axes = np.array(axes).reshape(-1)

pop_max = df['pop'].max()

for i, year in enumerate(years):
    ax = axes[i]
    sub = df[df['year'] == year]
    for cont in continents:
        d = sub[sub['continent'] == cont]
        sizes = (d['pop'] / pop_max) * 500 + 5
        ax.scatter(d['gdpPercap'], d['lifeExp'], s=sizes, c=[color_map[cont]],
                   alpha=0.6, edgecolors='white', linewidth=0.5, label=cont)
    ax.set_xscale('log')
    ax.set_title(f'year={year}')

for j in range(len(years), len(axes)):
    axes[j].axis('off')

for ax in axes[-ncols:]:
    ax.set_xlabel('gdpPercap')
for ax in axes[::ncols]:
    ax.set_ylabel('lifeExp')

handles = [plt.scatter([], [], c=[color_map[c]], label=c) for c in continents]
fig.legend(handles=handles, title='continent', loc='center right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.0, 0.5))
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0.9, 1])
plt.show()

instruction:
To generate the figure using the given Python code, you'll need a few essential pieces of information.

Data Requirements:
The data used for the plot is called 'tips'. This dataset comes pre-loaded with the Plotly Express library.

Figure Details:
- The figure generated is a Scatter plot.
- The x-axis represents the 'total bill' amount.
- The y-axis represents the 'tip' amount.
- Different colors are used to distinguish between the sex of the tipper.
- The figure is divided into multiple sub-plots (facets) based on the 'day' (across columns) and 'time' (across rows).

Line of Best Fit:
- A line is added to the scatter plot to represent the overall Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression model prediction for tip based on the total bill. 
- The line color is black and is hidden from the legend.
- This line is added to all facets except empty ones. 

Legend:
- Only the last trace added appears in the legend.
  
To perfectly recreate this figure, ensure you match these described specifications with the original code.
code_reference:
import plotly.express as px
df = px.data.tips()
fig = px.scatter(df, x="total_bill", y="tip", color='sex',
                 facet_col="day", facet_row="time")

import statsmodels.api as sm
import plotly.graph_objects as go
df = df.sort_values(by="total_bill")
model = sm.OLS(df["tip"], sm.add_constant(df["total_bill"])).fit()

#create the trace to be added to all facets
trace = go.Scatter(x=df["total_bill"], y=model.predict(),
                   line_color="black", name="overall OLS")

# give it a legend group and hide it from the legend
trace.update(legendgroup="trendline", showlegend=False)

# add it to all rows/cols, but not to empty subplots
fig.add_trace(trace, row="all", col="all", exclude_empty_subplots=True)

# set only the last trace added to appear in the legend
# `selector=-1` introduced in plotly v4.13
fig.update_traces(selector=-1, showlegend=True)
fig.show()
code:
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

tips = px.data.tips()

fig = px.scatter(tips, x="total_bill", y="tip", color="sex",
                 facet_col="day", facet_row="time")

x = tips["total_bill"].values
y = tips["tip"].values
m, b = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
x_line = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 100)
y_line = m * x_line + b

days = tips["day"].unique().tolist()
times = tips["time"].unique().tolist()

col_order = list(fig.layout.annotations)
facet_cols = {}
for ann in fig.layout.annotations:
    if "=" in ann.text:
        pass

# Determine facet structure from fig
row_vals = []
col_vals = []
for ann in fig.layout.annotations:
    txt = ann.text
    if txt.startswith("day="):
        col_vals.append(txt.split("=")[1])
    elif txt.startswith("time="):
        row_vals.append(txt.split("=")[1])

for i, t in enumerate(row_vals):
    for j, d in enumerate(col_vals):
        sub = tips[(tips["day"] == d) & (tips["time"] == t)]
        if len(sub) == 0:
            continue
        showlegend = (i == len(row_vals) - 1 and j == len(col_vals) - 1)
        fig.add_scatter(x=x_line, y=y_line, mode="lines",
                        line=dict(color="black"),
                        name="OLS", showlegend=showlegend,
                        row=len(row_vals) - i, col=j + 1)

fig.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

days = ["Thur", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"]
smokers = ["Yes", "No"]
sexes = ["Male", "Female"]
colors = {"Yes": "#636EFA", "No": "#EF553B"}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5), sharey=True)

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import math
try:
    from plotly.data import gapminder
    df = gapminder()
except Exception:
    import pandas as pd
    df = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminder_with_codes.csv")

df = df[df['continent'] == 'Africa']
countries = sorted(df['country'].unique())

ncols = 7
nrows = math.ceil(len(countries) / ncols)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows, ncols, figsize=(8, 6), sharex=True, sharey=True)
fig.subplots_adjust(hspace=0.04*nrows, wspace=0.04*ncols)

for i, country in enumerate(countries):
    ax = axes.flat[i]
    sub = df[df['country'] == country].sort_values('year')
    ax.plot(sub['year'], sub['lifeExp'])
    ax.set_title(country, fontsize=7)
    ax.tick_params(axis='both', labelsize=6)
    plt.setp(ax.get_yticklabels(), visible=True)

for j in range(len(countries), nrows*ncols):
    axes.flat[j].set_visible(False)

fig.suptitle("Life Expectancy in Africa")
fig.supxlabel("year")
fig.supylabel("lifeExp")
plt.show()

1. The data: The figure is created with 12 sets of normally distributed random data. The mean and standard deviation for this data increase for each set.

2. Random Seed: This is set to 1. This is crucial for recreating the figure because it ensures you would get the same sets of random data each time the code is run.

3. Colors: A range of 12 colors is used in the figure. These colors span from 'rgb(5, 200, 200)' to 'rgb(200, 10, 10)'.

4. Figure details: Violin plots are used in this figure with each having a different color from the range mentioned above. Each plot represents a line of data. The orientation of these plots is horizontal, they appear only on the positive side and their width is set to 3. Also, no points are shown on these plots.

5. In terms of layout, the x-axis grid and x-axis zero lines are not shown. 

Remember, the figure is created using plotly, so you would need that specific library to recreate it.
code_reference:
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.colors import n_colors
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(1)

# 12 sets of normal distributed random data, with increasing mean and standard deviation
data = (np.linspace(1, 2, 12)[:, np.newaxis] * np.random.randn(12, 200) +
            (np.arange(12) + 2 * np.random.random(12))[:, np.newaxis])

colors = n_colors('rgb(5, 200, 200)', 'rgb(200, 10, 10)', 12, colortype='rgb')

fig = go.Figure()
for data_line, color in zip(data, colors):
    fig.add_trace(go.Violin(x=data_line, line_color=color))

fig.update_traces(orientation='h', side='positive', width=3, points=False)
fig.update_layout(xaxis_showgrid=False, xaxis_zeroline=False)
fig.show()
code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(1)
n = 12
data = [np.random.normal(loc=i, scale=i+1, size=200) for i in range(n)]

def interp(c1, c2, t):
    return tuple(c1[k] + (c2[k]-c1[k])*t for k in range(3))

c1 = (5/255, 200/255, 200/255)
c2 = (200/255, 10/255, 10/255)
colors = [interp(c1, c2, i/(n-1)) for i in range(n)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
positions = list(range(n))
parts = ax.violinplot(data, positions=positions, vert=False, widths=3, showmeans=False, showmedians=False, showextrema=False)

for i, pc in enumerate(parts['bodies']):
    verts = pc.get_paths()[0].vertices
    ycenter = positions[i]
    verts[:,1] = np.where(verts[:,1] < ycenter, ycenter, verts[:,1])
    pc.set_facecolor(colors[i])
    pc.set_edgecolor(colors[i])
    pc.set_alpha(0.8)

ax.xaxis.grid(False)
ax.set_yticks(positions)
plt.show()

        line=dict(color='rgb(0,100,80)'),

        fill='toself',
        fillcolor='rgba(0,100,80,0.2)',
        line=dict(color='rgba(255,255,255,0)'),
        hoverinfo="skip",
        showlegend=False
    )
])

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
import pandas as pd

iris = load_iris()
df = pd.DataFrame(iris.data, columns=['sepal_length','sepal_width','petal_length','petal_width'])
df['species'] = [iris.target_names[i] for i in iris.target]

species_list = ['setosa','versicolor','virginica']
colors = {'setosa':'#636EFA','versicolor':'#EF553B','virginica':'#00CC96'}

fig, (ax_box, ax_hist) = plt.subplots(2, 1, sharex=True, gridspec_kw={'height_ratios':[1,4]}, figsize=(8,6))

bins = np.linspace(df['sepal_length'].min(), df['sepal_length'].max(), 20)
data = [df[df['species']==s]['sepal_length'].values for s in species_list]
ax_hist.hist(data, bins=bins, stacked=True, color=[colors[s] for s in species_list], label=species_list)
ax_hist.set_xlabel('sepal_length')
ax_hist.set_ylabel('count')
ax_hist.legend(title='species')

for i, s in enumerate(species_list):
    ax_box.boxplot(df[df['species']==s]['sepal_length'], positions=[i], vert=False,
                   patch_artist=True, boxprops=dict(facecolor=colors[s]), widths=0.6)
ax_box.set_yticks([])
ax_box.set_ylabel('')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

df = px.data.tips()
days = ['Thur', 'Fri', 'Sat', 'Sun']
colors = {'Male': 'tab:blue', 'Female': 'tab:red'}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, len(days), figsize=(16, 6), sharex=True, sharey='row',
                         gridspec_kw={'height_ratios': [1, 4]})

for i, day in enumerate(days):
    sub = df[df['day'] == day]
    ax_box = axes[0, i]
    ax_box.boxplot(sub['total_bill'], vert=False, widths=0.5)
    ax_box.set_yticks([])
    ax_box.set_title(day)

    ax = axes[1, i]
    for sex, c in colors.items():
        s = sub[sub['sex'] == sex]
        ax.scatter(s['total_bill'], s['tip'], c=c, label=sex, alpha=0.7, edgecolors='w')
    ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')
    if i == 0:
        ax.set_ylabel('tip')
    if i == len(days) - 1:
        ax.legend(title='sex')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

tips = px.data.tips()
days = ['Thur', 'Fri', 'Sat', 'Sun']
sexes = ['Male', 'Female']
colors = {'Male': '#636EFA', 'Female': '#EF553B'}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(16, 6), sharex='col',
                          gridspec_kw={'height_ratios': [1, 4], 'hspace': 0.05, 'wspace': 0.1})

bins = np.linspace(tips['total_bill'].min(), tips['total_bill'].max(), 20)

for i, day in enumerate(days):
    ax_box = axes[0, i]
    ax_hist = axes[1, i]
    sub = tips[tips['day'] == day]

    box_data = [sub[sub['sex'] == s]['total_bill'].values for s in sexes]
    bp = ax_box.boxplot(box_data, vert=False, patch_artist=True, widths=0.6)
    for patch, s in zip(bp['boxes'], sexes):
        patch.set_facecolor(colors[s])
    ax_box.set_yticks([])
    ax_box.set_title(f'day={day}')
    ax_box.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax_box.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

    bottom = np.zeros(len(bins) - 1)
    for s in sexes:
        vals = sub[sub['sex'] == s]['total_bill']
        counts, _ = np.histogram(vals, bins=bins)
        ax_hist.bar(bins[:-1], counts, width=np.diff(bins), bottom=bottom,
                    color=colors[s], align='edge', label=s if i == 0 else None)
        bottom += counts
    ax_hist.set_xlabel('total_bill')
    if i == 0:
        ax_hist.set_ylabel('count')

axes[1, 0].legend(title='sex')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

try:
    import plotly.express as px
    df = px.data.tips()
except Exception:
    np.random.seed(0)
    n = 244
    df = pd.DataFrame({
        'total_bill': np.random.gamma(4, 5, n),
        'tip': np.random.gamma(2, 1.5, n),
        'sex': np.random.choice(['Male', 'Female'], n, p=[0.64, 0.36]),
    })

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9, 7))
gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 1, height_ratios=[1, 5], hspace=0.05)
ax_box = fig.add_subplot(gs[0])
ax_hist = fig.add_subplot(gs[1], sharex=ax_box)

colors = {'Male': '#636EFA', 'Female': '#EF553B'}
sexes = ['Male', 'Female']

bins = np.linspace(df['total_bill'].min(), df['total_bill'].max(), 30)
bottom = np.zeros(len(bins) - 1)
for s in sexes:
    vals = df.loc[df['sex'] == s, 'total_bill']
    weights = df.loc[df['sex'] == s, 'tip']
    counts, _ = np.histogram(vals, bins=bins, weights=weights)
    ax_hist.bar(bins[:-1], counts, width=np.diff(bins), bottom=bottom,
                align='edge', color=colors[s], label=s, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.5)
    bottom += counts

ax_hist.set_xlabel('total_bill')
ax_hist.set_ylabel('sum of tip')
ax_hist.legend(title='sex')

data_box = [df.loc[df['sex'] == s, 'total_bill'].values for s in sexes]
bp = ax_box.boxplot(data_box, vert=False, positions=[1, 2], widths=0.6, patch_artist=True)
for patch, s in zip(bp['boxes'], sexes):
    patch.set_facecolor(colors[s])
ax_box.set_yticks([])
ax_box.tick_params(axis='x', labelbottom=False)
for sp in ['top', 'right', 'left']:
    ax_box.spines[sp].set_visible(False)

plt.show()

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import gaussian_kde

groups = [np.random.randn(200) - 2, np.random.randn(200), np.random.randn(200) + 2, np.random.randn(200) + 4]
labels = ['Group 1', 'Group 2', 'Group 3', 'Group 4']
bin_sizes = [0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1]
colors = ['#1f77b4', '#ff7f0e', '#2ca02c', '#d62728']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for data, label, bs, c in zip(groups, labels, bin_sizes, colors):
    bins = np.arange(data.min(), data.max() + bs, bs)
    ax.hist(data, bins=bins, density=True, alpha=0.5, color=c, label=label)
    kde = gaussian_kde(data)
    xs = np.linspace(data.min(), data.max(), 200)
    ax.plot(xs, kde(xs), color=c)
    ax.plot(data, np.zeros_like(data) - 0.01, '|', color=c)

ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2. Group Labels: The two groups of random numbers are labeled as '2014' and '2015'.

3. Rug Text: There are two sets of rug texts, one for each group. The first set consists of alphabets from 'a' to 'z', and the second from 'aa' to 'zz'.

4. Colors: The groups are colored in two different colors. The first group has a deep blue color represented by the RGB value (0, 0, 100), and the second group has a bright teal color represented by the RGB value (0, 200, 200).

import plotly.figure_factory as ff
import numpy as np

x1 = np.random.randn(26)
x2 = np.random.randn(26) + .5

group_labels = ['2014', '2015']

rug_text_one = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g', 'h', 'i', 'j',
                'k', 'l', 'm', 'n', 'o', 'p', 'q', 'r', 's', 't',
                'u', 'v', 'w', 'x', 'y', 'z']

import numpy as np

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

tips = sns.load_dataset('tips')

sexes = tips['sex'].unique()
smokers = tips['smoker'].unique()
colors = {'Yes': 'red', 'No': 'blue'}

        sub = tips[(tips['sex'] == s) & (tips['smoker'] == sm)]

    ax.set_ylabel('tip')
    ax.legend()

This Python code creates a complex figure using plotly, with the following major components:

The first function generates a data ranging from -1 to 1.2 with steps of size 2000. The second function introduces some randomness in the generated data using standard normal distribution. 3. The scatter plot: Superimposed on the 2D histogram contour is a scatter plot, which has markers of size '3' and color set as 'black with 0.3 alpha (semi-transparent)'.

5. The layout: The final layout of the figure is set such that four different plots are created within the same space. The main 2D histogram contour and the scatter plot take up 85% of the x and y axes, leaving the rest of the space to the histograms. These plots without grid lines, with a figure size of 600x600 pixels, and a zero-sized gap between histogram bars. The figure is set to report the closest data on hover and legends are not displayed.

6. Randomness: The code uses a random seed introduced by the standard normal distribution in numpy. This influences the output data 'x' and 'y' and therefore the final plot. The seed is not explicitly specified in code, it will need to be fixed for the figures to be reproducible.

To recreate this figure, you would need the raw data used in the figure, which include the two sets of data 'x' and 'y', the color specification, size and distribution of the data on the plot, and the specific instructions on how the plot were laid out, the domains for each plot, and the dimensions of the overall figure.

import plotly.graph_objects as go

import numpy as np

t = np.linspace(-1, 1.2, 2000)
x = (t**3) + (0.3 * np.random.randn(2000))
y = (t**6) + (0.3 * np.random.randn(2000))

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram2dContour(
        x = x,
        y = y,
        colorscale = 'Blues',
        reversescale = True,
        xaxis = 'x',
        yaxis = 'y'
    ))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x = x,
        y = y,
        xaxis = 'x',
        yaxis = 'y',
        mode = 'markers',
        marker = dict(
            color = 'rgba(0,0,0,0.3)',
            size = 3
        )
    ))
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
        y = y,
        xaxis = 'x2',
        marker = dict(
            color = 'rgba(0,0,0,1)'
        )
    ))
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
        x = x,
        yaxis = 'y2',
        marker = dict(
            color = 'rgba(0,0,0,1)'
        )
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    autosize = False,
    xaxis = dict(
        zeroline = False,
        domain = [0,0.85],
        showgrid = False
    ),
    yaxis = dict(
        zeroline = False,
        domain = [0,0.85],
        showgrid = False
    ),
    xaxis2 = dict(
        zeroline = False,
        domain = [0.85,1],
        showgrid = False
    ),
    yaxis2 = dict(
        zeroline = False,
        domain = [0.85,1],
        showgrid = False
    ),
    height = 600,
    width = 600,
    bargap = 0,
    hovermode = 'closest',
    showlegend = False
)

fig.show()

The Python code generates a box-and-whisker plot using the Plotly library, specifically the interactive Plotly Express module. The data used for the graph is the "tips" dataset, commonly provided by Plotly Express for educational purposes.

Looking closer at the plot, the x-axis represents the days of the week, while the y-axis showcases the total bill amount. Each box in the plot corresponds to a unique day from the data and shows the statistical quartiles of the total bill for that day. More specifically, the box represents the Inter Quartile Range (IQR) with the line in the box as the median, and the whiskers - the lines extending from the box - marking the highest and the lowest values.

In addition to this, the boxes are color-coded based on whether the party included a smoker or not, creating two boxes (one for smoker, one for non-smoker) for each unique day. 

The computation of the quartiles of each box is exclusive. This means that the computation does not include the median to compute the lower and upper quartiles. This is customizable as inclusive, linear, or kept to default as shown in the code. 

All this information together creates a clear and interactive box-and-whisker plot that displays the variations of the total bill paid each day, split by smoker and non-smoker groups.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
days = ["Thur", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"]
colors = {"Yes": "#EF553B", "No": "#636EFA"}
width = 0.35

for i, day in enumerate(days):
    for j, sm in enumerate(["Yes", "No"]):
        data = tips[(tips["day"] == day) & (tips["smoker"] == sm)]["total_bill"].values
        pos = i + (j - 0.5) * width
        bp = ax.boxplot(data, positions=[pos], widths=width*0.9, patch_artist=True,
                        boxprops=dict(facecolor=colors[sm], edgecolor=colors[sm]),
                        medianprops=dict(color="white"),
                        whiskerprops=dict(color=colors[sm]),
                        capprops=dict(color=colors[sm]),
                        flierprops=dict(markeredgecolor=colors[sm]))

ax.set_xticks(range(len(days)))
ax.set_xticklabels(days)
ax.set_xlabel("day")
ax.set_ylabel("total_bill")

from matplotlib.patches import Patch
handles = [Patch(color=colors["Yes"], label="Yes"), Patch(color=colors["No"], label="No")]
ax.legend(handles=handles, title="smoker")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

data = [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]
df = pd.DataFrame(dict(
    linear=data,
    inclusive=data,
    exclusive=data
)).melt(var_name="quartilemethod")

             boxmode="overlay", points='all')

fig.update_traces(quartilemethod="exclusive", jitter=0, col=3)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

tips = px.data.tips()

times = tips['time'].unique()
smokers = tips['smoker'].unique()
colors = {'Yes': '#EF553B', 'No': '#636EFA'}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

positions = []
data = []
box_colors = []
labels_pos = []

width = 0.35
for i, t in enumerate(times):
    for j, s in enumerate(smokers):
        d = tips[(tips['time'] == t) & (tips['smoker'] == s)]['total_bill'].values
        pos = i + (j - 0.5) * width * 1.1
        positions.append(pos)
        data.append(d)
        box_colors.append(colors[s])

bp = ax.boxplot(data, positions=positions, widths=width, notch=True, patch_artist=True)
for patch, c in zip(bp['boxes'], box_colors):
    patch.set_facecolor(c)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(times)))
ax.set_xticklabels(times)
ax.set_xlabel('time')
ax.set_ylabel('total_bill')
ax.set_title('Box plot of total bill')

from matplotlib.patches import Patch
legend_elements = [Patch(facecolor=colors[s], label=s) for s in smokers]
ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, title='smoker')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = [0.75, 5.25, 5.5, 6, 6.2, 6.6, 6.80, 7.0, 7.2, 7.5, 7.5, 7.75, 8.15, 8.15, 8.65, 8.93, 9.2, 9.5, 10, 10.25, 11.5, 12, 16, 20.90, 22.3, 23.25]

labels = ['All Points', 'Only Whiskers', 'Suspected Outliers', 'Whiskers and Outliers']

import numpy as np

# 1: all points

                 flierprops=dict(marker='o', markerfacecolor=(219/255,64/255,82/255,0.6),

ax.set_xticks(positions)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(0)
days = ['day 1', 'day 2']
veggies = ['kale', 'radishes', 'carrots']
colors = ['#3D9970', '#FF4136', '#FF851B']

data = {v: [np.random.rand(20) for _ in days] for v in veggies}

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
width = 0.25
positions = np.arange(len(days))

for i, v in enumerate(veggies):
    pos = positions + (i - 1) * width
    bp = ax.boxplot(data[v], positions=pos, widths=width*0.9, patch_artist=True)
    for patch in bp['boxes']:
        patch.set_facecolor(colors[i])
    for median in bp['medians']:
        median.set_color('black')

ax.set_xticks(positions)
ax.set_xticklabels(days)
ax.set_ylabel('normalized moisture')
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)

handles = [plt.Rectangle((0,0),1,1, color=c) for c in colors]
ax.legend(handles, veggies)

plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

The figure is a box plot, which is a statistical representation of numerical data through their quartiles. The number of boxes in the plot is 30. 

Each box has a unique color, generated using the hue, saturation, and lightness (HSL) representation of color. The saturation and lightness are set at 50%, while the hue varies from 0 to 360. 

The y-values are computed using a sinusoidal function combined with random numbers, producing distinct data for each box. As these values are generated randomly, the exact numerical data will vary each time the code is run.

The layout of the figure is formatted to hide grids on the x-axis and the zero lines on both axes. The colors of the paper background and plot are set to 'rgb(233,233,233)'. 

To recreate the plot, you will need to generate 30 different data sets, with each set generated using the sinusoidal function mentioned above. Each data set will be represented by a separate box on the box plot. 

Overall, you will need:
1. A box plot with 30 boxes.
2. Unique colors for each box using the HSL color range.
3. Generate y-values using the provided mathematical formula. 
4. Hide grids on the x-axis and the zero-line on both axes.
5. Set the background colors of the paper and plot to 'rgb(233,233,233)'.

import plotly.graph_objects as go
import numpy as np

N = 30     # Number of boxes

# generate an array of rainbow colors by fixing the saturation and lightness of the HSL
# representation of colour and marching around the hue.
# Plotly accepts any CSS color format, see e.g. http://www.w3schools.com/cssref/css_colors_legal.asp.
c = ['hsl('+str(h)+',50%'+',50%)' for h in np.linspace(0, 360, N)]

# Each box is represented by a dict that contains the data, the type, and the colour.
# Use list comprehension to describe N boxes, each with a different colour and with different randomly generated data:
fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Box(
    y=3.5 * np.sin(np.pi * i/N) + i/N + (1.5 + 0.5 * np.cos(np.pi*i/N)) * np.random.rand(10),
    marker_color=c[i]
    ) for i in range(int(N))])

# format the layout
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False),
    yaxis=dict(zeroline=False, gridcolor='white'),
    paper_bgcolor='rgb(233,233,233)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgb(233,233,233)',
)

fig.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import colorsys

np.random.seed(0)
N = 30
data = []
colors = []
for i in range(N):
    y = np.sin(np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 50) + i) + np.random.randn(50)
    data.append(y)
    colors.append(colorsys.hls_to_rgb(i/N, 0.5, 0.5))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12,6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor((233/255,233/255,233/255))
ax.set_facecolor((233/255,233/255,233/255))

bp = ax.boxplot(data, patch_artist=True)
for patch, c in zip(bp['boxes'], colors):
    patch.set_facecolor(c)

ax.xaxis.grid(False)
ax.yaxis.grid(True)
ax.axhline(0, color=(233/255,233/255,233/255))
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

try:
    import plotly.express as px
    df = px.data.tips()
except Exception:
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

ax.set_xticklabels(order)
ax.set_xlabel("day")
ax.set_ylabel("tip")

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

    ax.set_title(f"smoker={sm}")

    ax.legend(title="sex")

axes[0].set_ylabel("tip")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

smokers = tips['smoker'].unique()

import pandas as pd

    from plotly.data import gapminder
    df = gapminder()

    y = df['lifeExp'].values

xs = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 200)
ys = coef[0] * np.log(xs) + coef[1]
ax.plot(xs, ys, color='red')

ax.set_xlabel('gdpPercap')
ax.set_ylabel('lifeExp')
ax.set_title('Log-transformed fit on linear axes')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import statsmodels.api as sm
import pandas as pd

df = px.data.stocks()
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.scatter(df['date'], df['GOOG'], alpha=0.7, label='GOOG')

x_num = df['date'].map(pd.Timestamp.toordinal).values
smoothed = sm.nonparametric.lowess(df['GOOG'].values, x_num, frac=0.3)
ax.plot([pd.Timestamp.fromordinal(int(x)) for x in smoothed[:, 0]], smoothed[:, 1], color='red', label='lowess')

ax.set_xlabel('date')
ax.set_ylabel('GOOG')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

You will then create a scatter plot with a rolling window of 5 for the trendline, while ensuring that only trendlines, not individual data points, are displayed on the plot. import plotly.express as px

df = px.data.stocks(indexed=True, datetimes=True)
fig = px.scatter(df, trendline="rolling", trendline_options=dict(window=5),

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
try:
    import plotly.express as px
    df = px.data.tips()
    total_bill = df['total_bill'].values
    tip = df['tip'].values

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px

df = px.data.tips()

sexes = ['Male', 'Female']
smokers = ['Yes', 'No']

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 8), sharex=True, sharey=True)

xbins = np.linspace(df['total_bill'].min(), df['total_bill'].max(), 20)
ybins = np.linspace(df['tip'].min(), df['tip'].max(), 20)

for i, sex in enumerate(sexes):
    for j, smoker in enumerate(smokers):
        ax = axes[i, j]
        sub = df[(df['sex'] == sex) & (df['smoker'] == smoker)]
        h = ax.hist2d(sub['total_bill'], sub['tip'], bins=[xbins, ybins], cmap='Plasma')
        ax.set_title(f'sex={sex}, smoker={smoker}')
        if i == 1:
            ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')
        if j == 0:
            ax.set_ylabel('tip')
        fig.colorbar(h[3], ax=ax)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px

tips = px.data.tips()
x = tips['total_bill'].values
y = tips['tip'].values

H, xedges, yedges = np.histogram2d(x, y, bins=10)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
im = ax.imshow(H.T, origin='lower', aspect='auto',
               extent=[xedges[0], xedges[-1], yedges[0], yedges[-1]],
               cmap='viridis')

for i in range(H.shape[0]):
    for j in range(H.shape[1]):
        val = H[i, j]
        if val > 0:
            xc = (xedges[i] + xedges[i+1]) / 2
            yc = (yedges[j] + yedges[j+1]) / 2
            ax.text(xc, yc, int(val), ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontsize=9)

ax.set_xlabel('total_bill')
ax.set_ylabel('tip')
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label='count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
